mappings.rationaleAI: Music as a cultural universal covering composition, performance, elements (pitch, melody, harmony, rhythm, timbre), notation, recording, and history comprehensively addresses the entire outline of the art of music.
Singing as the creation of music through the human voice with vocal classification and techniques directly addresses the human voice (B.2) and vocal music (E.1.b).
Theatre as performing art incorporating music connects to music for the theatre including opera and musical theatre (E.3.c).

Bach's mastery of counterpoint and fugue addresses counterpoint (C.5) and the fugue as a compositional form (E.2.a.v), while his Baroque sacred and instrumental works connect to Western music history (G.1).

Beethoven's symphonies, concertos, and sonatas address the sonata (E.2.a.i), symphony (E.2.a.ii), and concerto (E.2.a.iii), while his pivotal role bridging Classical and Romantic connects to Western music history (G.1).
Popular culture's dominant musical forms circulated through mass media address genres determined by cultural milieu including popular music (E.4).
Dance's connection to musical accompaniment addresses the relation of music to other human activities (A.4) and music for theatrical dance (E.3.c.ii).
Opera as musical theatre with vocal and orchestral forms directly addresses opera as music for the theatre (E.3.c.i).
Performing arts as live audience arts connect to musical performance and interpretation (A.3).
The history of music from prehistoric origins through modern genres directly addresses the history of music (G).
Rhythm as regulated succession of strong and weak elements directly addresses duration and time (C.2) and rhythm as a musical element (C.2.b).
The orchestra's organization into strings, woodwinds, brass, and percussion addresses musical instruments (B.3) and orchestration and instrumentation (C.7).
Film's use of music for scoring connects to incidental and background music for motion pictures (E.3.c.iv).
Armstrong's pioneering of jazz improvisation and scat singing directly addresses jazz as music combining notation with extemporaneous performance (E.2.b) and connects to Western music history (G.1).
The Beatles' multi-track recording innovations and global commercial success address popular music (E.4) and recording and reproduction of music (F).
Pop music as a commercially driven genre with repeated choruses and hook-driven structures directly addresses popular music (E.4).
Musical instruments spanning 40,000 years of development with Hornbostel-Sachs classification directly address musical instruments and their types (B.3).
Classical music's formal complexity in symphonies and concertos addresses fully notated structures (E.2.a), liturgical music (E.3.a), and Western music history (G.1).
Television as a broadcast medium connects to incidental and background music for television (E.3.c.iv).
Emotion as neurophysiological states associated with experience connects to problems of musical meaning (A.2).
Musicology as academic research-based study encompassing historical, systematic, and ethnomusicological branches directly addresses writings about music (A.5).
Rock music originating in African-American blues and evolving into diverse subgenres directly addresses popular music (E.4).
Telecommunications' progression from telegraph through radio and digital media connects to types of music reproduction (F.1).
Folklore's musical traditions transmitted orally address primitive, folk, and popular music (E.4).
Oral tradition's transmission of musical practices across generations addresses primitive and folk music (E.4).
